headlineSummary: OpenAI drew the most negative sentiment on trust and privacy concerns, Google Gemini was also negative on limited evidence, and Anthropic was viewed more positively for trust and privacy despite some complaints about limits [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6][E7].

ranking.summary: OpenAI was criticized for weak ethics, privacy concerns, and trust issues, with one comment still preferring its pricing/usage over alternatives [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6].
Google Gemini had negative sentiment on limited evidence, driven by a privacy-related cancellation comment that grouped it with ChatGPT [E4].
Anthropic was generally positive, with praise for stronger safeguards, a favorable trust judgment, and a good coding experience, though one comment complained about tight limits [E1][E2][E4][E6][E7].

evidence.excerpt: We do not think Anthropic should be designated as a supply chain risk

annotations.rationale: The title directly argues Anthropic should not be treated as a supply chain risk, which is a favorable trust judgment.

evidence.excerpt: both want the same contract terms, but Antropic wants to enforce those terms via technology, and OpenAI wants to enforce them by ... telling the Government not to violate them. It's telling that the government is blacklisting the company that wants to do more than enforce the contract with words on paper.

annotations.rationale: The commenter portrays Anthropic as taking stronger concrete safeguards by enforcing terms technologically.
OpenAI is criticized as relying only on unenforced promises to the government rather than technical safeguards.

evidence.excerpt: Very much feels like OpenAI trying to PR manage their weaker ethical stance

annotations.rationale: This directly accuses OpenAI of PR spin around an ethically weaker position.

evidence.excerpt: I canceled my subscriptions to ChatGPT and Gemini yesterday over this and switched to Claude. I know $20 isn’t much, But to me not willing to spy on me for the US government is a good market differentiator.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they canceled ChatGPT over the issue and contrasts it with not wanting a provider to spy for the US government.
Gemini is explicitly included among the canceled subscriptions over the same spying-related concern.
Switching to Claude is framed positively because the commenter sees not spying for the US government as a favorable differentiator.

evidence.excerpt: I switched because I have come to believe OpenAI are a bad actor and I do not want to support them in any way. I’m pretty sure they would allow AGI to be used for truly evil purposes, and the events of this week have only convinced me further.

annotations.rationale: The commenter calls OpenAI a bad actor and says they believe it would allow AGI to be used for evil purposes.

evidence.excerpt: I switched to Claude but the token efficiency and limits are much more noticeable. One or two coding questions and I'm at my session limit. And that is shared with chat too. I was mostly able to get by with $20 codex but I'll probably have to splurge for the Max plan.

annotations.rationale: The commenter complains that Claude's limits are restrictive enough to hit the session cap after only one or two coding questions.
The comparison says $20 Codex was mostly sufficient, implying a more favorable cost/usage experience than Claude's Max plan.

evidence.excerpt: I've had a pretty good experience using Claude with a framework where I express the program as a state graph, and each node is treated like a microservice that gets some input and produces some output.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly reports a pretty good experience using Claude in a coding workflow.
